description: The provided image is an irrelevant user interface element displaying a "Check for updates" button with a blue-yellow circular logo and red bookmark icon. No content related to the research on Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C ferrite-based low density steel, warm temperature deformation induced bi-directional ferrite-austenite transformation, microstructure characterization, mechanical performance testing, or thermodynamic calculation results from the target paper is identifiable. No valid material science research related information can be extracted from this unrelated visual content, which is likely a result of incorrect image upload or loading failure.

This figure presents the initial microstructure and warm compressive mechanical properties of Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel after annealing at 950 °C for 1 h. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD phase map, visualizing the triplex microstructure: green represents ferrite, yellow represents martensite, red represents austenite, with a scale bar of 50 μm. Subfigure (b) is a TEM bright-field micrograph accompanied by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, identifying γ (austenite, region 1, Z=[001] zone axis) and α' (martensite, region 2, with Z=[0\=011] and [-111] zone axes) phases, with white arrows marking initial ferrite-ferrite grain boundaries and a 1 μm scale bar. Subfigures (c) and (d) show mechanical test results: (c) displays true stress-true strain curves tested at 500 °C and 600 °C under 0.001 s⁻¹ strain rate, where maximum flow stress reaches ~570 MPa at 500 °C and ~400 MPa at 600 °C, with obvious flow softening after true strain of 0.3 at both temperatures; (d) plots maximum flow stress and 0.2% yield stress against deformation temperature from 100 to 700 °C, showing stable stress levels at 200-500 °C and a sharp stress drop above 500 °C (marked by blue dashed oval), which is attributed to strain-induced phase transformation. The figure confirms the triplex initial microstructure of the studied steel, and reveals the temperature dependence of its warm deformation mechanical response.

This set of figures show EBSD phase maps (PM) overlaid on image quality (IQ) maps and corresponding separated phase maps of Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel compressed to true strain 0.6 at strain rate 0.001 s⁻¹. Subfigure (a) is the IQ-overlaid PM for the sample deformed at 500 °C, where green refers to BCC ferrite and red refers to FCC austenite, with densely distributed deformation bands observed. Subfigure (b) is the IQ-overlaid PM for the sample deformed at 600 °C, with compression axis (CA) direction marked at the top right, no obvious deformation bands detected due to more uniform strain distribution at higher temperature. Subfigures (c) and (d) are separated three-phase PMs corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively, with yellow added to represent martensite, all subfigures share a 20 μm scale bar. The microstructure difference confirms that strain localization in deformation bands formed at 500 °C provides driving force for bidirectional strain-induced ferrite-austenite transformation, while both deformation conditions retain triplex (ferrite, austenite, martensite) microstructure after deformation, providing direct microscopic evidence for the bidirectional transformation mechanism during warm deformation.

This figure presents microstructural characterizations of Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel after warm deformation. Subfigure (a) is a TEM bright-field image of ferrite phase from samples deformed at 500 °C to interrupted true strain of 0.3, showing tangled dislocations, high dislocation density walls (HDDWs), and {110} & {112} primary/secondary slip traces. Subfigure (b) is a TEM bright-field image of the same deformation condition, revealing fresh ferrite grains (marked by white arrows) formed inside parent austenite islands, with corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirming ferrite (zone axis [113]) and austenite (zone axis [111]) phases. Subfigures (c-d) are high-magnification phase maps (green: ferrite, red: austenite) of samples deformed at 500 °C to true strain 0.6, showing austenite shearing by deformation bands and bidirectional deformation-induced transformations along deformation bands. Subfigure (e) is the phase map of sample deformed at 600 °C, where no such transformation features are observed. This figure proves bidirectional phase transformation acts as a strain compensation mechanism, inducing flow softening and improving material workability.

This figure presents Thermo-Calc thermodynamic calculation results for the Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel system. Subplots (a) and (b) show the equilibrium mass percentages of Mn and Al respectively in BCC_A2 (ferrite), FCC_A1 (austenite) and FCC_A1#2 phases as a function of temperature from 0 to 1600 °C, indicating Mn preferentially partitions into FCC phase while Al prefers BCC phase, with phase compositions marked at the 1000 °C annealing temperature. Subplots (c, e) plot Gibbs free energy of BCC and FCC phases against Mn content (6-20 wt%) at 500 °C and 550 °C isothermal sections, while (d, f) plot Gibbs free energy against Al content (2-10 wt%) at the same temperatures. Point A marks the composition where BCC and FCC have equal Gibbs free energy, points B and C correspond to actual FCC and BCC compositions after elemental partitioning. After considering partitioning, BCC and FCC show nearly equal Gibbs free energy at 500 °C, providing thermodynamic basis for bidirectional deformation-induced ferrite-austenite transformation. Mn content dominates phase stability, while Al content has negligible effect on phase free energy difference.
